title: Dog Paw Pad Peeling: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's paw pad skin peeling? Learn about potential causes, the medium urgency level of this condition, and why photos help vets triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Paw pad peeling is a painful condition requiring veterinary evaluation within 24-48 hours to prevent infection. Providing a clear photo helps the clinical team assess the severity of the injury and determine the urgency of care.

What causes a dog's paw pads to peel?

Paw pad peeling occurs when the tough, keratinized outer layer of a dog's footpad separates from the sensitive tissue underneath. This condition can be caused by physical trauma, such as running on rough surfaces, or environmental factors like thermal burns from hot pavement and chemical burns from ice-melt salts. In some cases, it may also indicate underlying health issues like hyperkeratosis, autoimmune diseases, or fungal infections.

Is a peeling paw pad an emergency for my dog?

The urgency level for peeling paw pads is classified as Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is often very painful and carries a high risk of secondary infection. You should contact your veterinarian for an appointment within 24 to 48 hours, or sooner if:

  • Your dog is unable to bear weight
  • The area is bleeding profusely
  • There is visible pus

How does a photo of my dog's paw help with triage?

Capturing a clear, high-resolution photo of the affected paw is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A photograph allows a professional to perform the following:

  • Assess the depth of the tissue loss
  • Look for signs of inflammation or infection
  • Determine if the peeling is localized to one paw or systemic
  • Prioritize your dog's care and provide a baseline to monitor if the condition worsens

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Footpad peeling in dogs can be a manifestation of various underlying conditions. In young Chinese Shar-Pei, a severe form of neutrophilic vasculitis may present with footpad swelling, depigmentation, and ulceration, potentially affecting all footpads. Familial dermatomyositis in Collies and Shetland Sheepdogs can also involve skin and muscle inflammation. The severity of pad lesions may necessitate euthanasia in severe cases. A thorough dermatologic examination is crucial to document lesion type and distribution, and identify the underlying cause, considering factors like previous medication response, zoonotic potential, and environmental influences.

Chapter: Dermatology, Immunology, Genetics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 851)

faqs.question: Should I peel off the loose skin myself?
faqs.answer: No, you should never pull or peel loose skin from the paw pad, as this can tear healthy tissue and cause significant pain or bleeding. Let a veterinarian trim any flaps safely.

faqs.question: Can I use human antiseptic on my dog's paw?
faqs.answer: Avoid using human medications like Neosporin or alcohol without vet approval, as dogs frequently lick their paws and may ingest ingredients that are harmful to them.

faqs.question: How can I protect the paw until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: You can place a clean, breathable cotton sock over the paw and secure it loosely with medical tape (above the joint) to prevent your dog from licking and to keep the area clean.
